en.extract:
The Rodrigues fody (Foudia flavicans) is a rare species of bird in the weaver family. It is endemic to Rodrigues, an island of Mauritius. It is classified by BirdLife International as being vulnerable. It is also on the United States' Endangered Species List with an endangered status.
This bird is 12 to 13 cm (4.7 to 5.1 in) long. It is yellow with an orange face and brown back, wings, and tail.
The bird can be found in the canopy at heights of at least 5 metres (16 ft), and sometimes travels in flocks. It may be associated with the Norfolk Island pine (Araucaria cunninghamii) and some introduced species of plants. More birds are found in areas with more dense stands of taller trees and higher diversity of tree species. It feeds on insects, spiders, seeds, nectar, and fruit.
Once abundant on the island, the bird was nearly driven to extinction with only 5 or 6 pairs remaining by 1968. Due to conservation efforts its numbers rose to between 911 and 1200 individuals by the year 1999.
The main threat to the species is the destruction of its forest habitat. The forest was cleared for subsistence farming and livestock. The bird also competes with its introduced relative, the Madagascar red fody (Foudia madagascariensis). Cyclones and drought reduce the food supply.
Conservation efforts have included reforestation, the main cause of the increase in fody numbers. Fences keep out livestock.

The '''Rodrigues fody''' ('''''Foudia flavicans''''') is a rare species of [[bird]] in the [[Ploceidae|weaver family]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Rodrigues]], an island of [[Mauritius]]. It is classified by [[BirdLife International]] as being vulnerable.<ref name=birdlife>{{cite web|url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/rodrigues-fody-foudia-flavicans |publisher=BirdLife International |title=BirdLife Species Fact Sheet}}</ref> It is also on the United States' Endangered Species List with an endangered status.<ref name=fws>{{cite web|publisher=USFWS |url=http://ecos.fws.gov/docs/federal_register/fr2771.pdf |title=Addition of 30 African birds to list of endangered and threatened wildlife|website=Federal Register|date=January 12, 1995}}</ref>

This bird is {{cvt|12|to|13|cm}} long. It is yellow with an orange face and brown back, wings, and tail.<ref name=birdlife/>

The bird can be found in the canopy at heights of at least {{convert|5|m}}, and sometimes travels in flocks. It may be associated with the [[Araucaria cunninghamii|Norfolk Island pine]] (''Araucaria cunninghamii'') and some [[introduced species]] of plants. More birds are found in areas with more dense stands of taller trees and higher diversity of tree species.<ref name=impey>{{cite journal|author=Andrew J. Impey |author2=Isabelle M Côté|author3=Carl G Jones |display-authors=1 |year=2002 |url=http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S000632070200068X |title=Population recovery of the threatened endemic Rodrigues fody (''Foudia flavicans'') (Aves, Ploceidae) following reforestation |journal=Biological Conservation|volume=107|issue=3|pages=299–305|doi=10.1016/S0006-3207(02)00068-X |bibcode=2002BCons.107..299I |url-access=subscription }}</ref> It feeds on insects, spiders, seeds, nectar, and fruit.<ref name=birdlife/>

Once abundant on the island, the bird was nearly driven to extinction with only 5 or 6 pairs remaining by 1968. Due to conservation efforts its numbers rose to between 911 and 1200 individuals by the year 1999.<ref name=birdlife/>

The main threat to the species is the destruction of its forest habitat. The forest was cleared for [[subsistence farming]] and [[livestock]]. The bird also [[competition (biology)|competes]] with its introduced relative, the [[red fody|Madagascar red fody]] (''Foudia madagascariensis''). [[Cyclone]]s and [[drought]] reduce the food supply.<ref name=birdlife/>

Conservation efforts have included [[reforestation]], the main cause of the increase in fody numbers. [[Fence]]s keep out livestock.<ref name=birdlife/>
